industry.headline: MNRE Extends Self-Certification Timeline for Large Solar Inverters
industry.summary: The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) has extended the implementation timeline for mandatory compliance under the Solar Systems, Devices and Components Goods Order, 2025. This extension specifically applies to solar inverters with capacities above 200 kW. The move adjusts the regulatory schedule for self-certification requirements for these high-capacity components.

tech.headline: Avangrid Begins Oregon Trail Solar Panel Installation
tech.summary: Avangrid, an Iberdrola Group company, started panel installation at the 57-MW Oregon Trail Solar project in Gilliam County, Oregon. The milestone supports 200 local union jobs and marks progress in the project’s construction. The solar farm is part of Avangrid’s expansion of renewable energy infrastructure in the US. The project is expected to contribute to regional clean energy goals.

tech.headline: Peak Energy to build 4-GWh sodium-ion ESS plant in Sacramento
tech.summary: Peak Energy will establish a grid-scale sodium-ion energy storage manufacturing facility in Sacramento, California, with a 4-GWh annual capacity. The company has secured over 6 GWh in customer commitments and plans to begin production and shipments soon.

tech.headline: Solar recycling needs material diversity for viability
tech.summary: Researchers from Australia and Poland found end-of-life solar module recycling unviable without considering material diversity. The study focused on upcycling aluminium, glass and solar cells for reuse in manufacturing.

tech.headline: Iceland pilot achieves $1.75/kg geothermal hydrogen
tech.summary: Syntholene's geothermal-integrated SOEC hydrogen platform in Iceland could produce hydrogen at $1.75–$2.10/kg, one-third the cost of unsubsidized European green hydrogen. The project aims to validate low-emission production and address risks like electricity costs and electrolyzer durability.

tech.headline: Aggreko shifts LA service center to 100% solar
tech.summary: Aggreko completed an 82-kW solar array at its Los Angeles service center, transitioning to 100% on-site renewable, off-grid energy. This marks a key step in the company's sustainability campaign to power facilities with clean energy.

learn.title: Concentrated Solar Power Basics
learn.intro: Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) captures sunlight with mirrors to produce high‑temperature heat, which drives turbines for clean electricity. As the world seeks dispatchable renewables, CSP offers storage‑ready power that can run round‑the‑clock, crucial for meeting growing energy demand while cutting emissions.
learn.c1_text: CSP plants use rows of flat or curved mirrors to focus sunlight onto a receiver, similar to a giant magnifying glass. A 1 MW plant can concentrate the sun’s energy onto a 10‑meter tower, reaching temperatures above 400 °C, enough to boil water and spin a turbine.
learn.c2_text: The captured heat creates steam that drives a conventional turbine, just like a fossil‑fuel plant but without combustion. Modern designs use molten salt as a thermal storage medium, allowing the plant to generate electricity for up to 10 hours after sunset.
learn.c3_text: India’s Ministry of New & Renewable Energy targets 10 GW of CSP by 2030, with projects in Rajasthan and Gujarat. The country’s high solar insolation (>5.5 kWh/m²/day) makes CSP a viable complement to its expanding PV fleet.
learn.stat3: Thermal storage enables up to 10 h of dispatchable power
learn.stat4: India aims for 10 GW CSP capacity by 2030

learn.title: Vehicle‑to‑Grid (V2G) Essentials
learn.intro: Vehicle‑to‑grid (V2G) lets electric cars feed stored energy back to the grid, turning millions of mobile batteries into a flexible power reserve. This technology can smooth peaks, support renewables, and create new revenue streams for EV owners, accelerating the clean‑energy transition.
learn.c1_text: A V2G‑enabled charger works like a two‑way street: it can draw electricity to charge a car and push electricity back when the grid needs it. A typical 7 kW home charger can supply up to 3 kW to the grid without draining the vehicle’s range.
learn.c2_text: Aggregated EV fleets provide frequency regulation, peak shaving, and backup power. By responding within seconds, V2G can replace costly fossil‑fuel peaker plants, helping integrate solar and wind that are variable by nature.
learn.c3_text: India’s Delhi Metro and Tata Power are testing V2G with 1,000 electric buses, aiming to supply 5 MW of ancillary services to the local grid by 2025, aligning with the country’s target of 30 GW EV charging infrastructure.
learn.stat2: Potential V2G capacity: 1 TW if 10 % of EVs participate
learn.stat3: Frequency regulation revenue: $10‑20 per kWh exported
learn.stat4: India plans 30 GW EV charging network by 2030

learn.title: Wind Resource Assessment Steps
learn.intro: Finding the wind that will spin turbines efficiently requires careful resource assessment. Accurate data reduces financial risk, ensures optimal turbine placement, and maximizes clean energy output—key for scaling wind power worldwide.
learn.c1_text: Developers install anemometers on 30‑meter towers for at least a year to capture wind speed, direction, and turbulence. A single site can produce 2–3 MW of power per 100 m of rotor diameter under average 7 m/s wind speeds.
learn.c2_text: Measured data feed into mesoscale models (e.g., WRF) that extrapolate wind fields over larger areas. These models predict annual energy production (AEP) with ±10 % accuracy, guiding turbine layout and hub‑height decisions.
learn.c3_text: India’s Ministry of New & Renewable Energy identifies 13 wind corridors, with Gujarat’s Kutch region offering average wind speeds of 8.5 m/s—supporting 10 GW of installed capacity as of 2024.
learn.stat1: World offshore wind potential: 420 GW by 2050
learn.stat4: Typical capacity factor: 30‑45 % for onshore sites

learn.title: Green Hydrogen: PEM vs Alkaline
learn.intro: Green hydrogen, produced by splitting water with renewable electricity, can decarbonize industry, transport, and heating. Two main electrolyser technologies—Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) and alkaline—offer different trade‑offs in efficiency, cost, and scalability.
learn.c1_text: Alkaline cells use a liquid potassium hydroxide electrolyte and nickel electrodes. They are mature, cost‑effective (~$500/kW) and operate at 60‑80 °C, achieving 65‑70 % efficiency—similar to a gasoline engine’s fuel conversion.
learn.c2_text: PEM units employ a solid polymer membrane, run at 50‑80 °C, and reach 70‑80 % efficiency. They respond quickly to renewable power fluctuations and support higher current densities, enabling compact, modular plants.
learn.c3_text: India’s National Hydrogen Mission targets 5 GW of electrolyser capacity by 2030, with a focus on alkaline technology for large‑scale fertilizer hubs in Gujarat and PEM for pilot projects in Karnataka’s solar parks.
learn.stat4: India aims for 10 Mt of green H₂ annually by 2030

learn.title: Pumped Hydro Storage Explained
learn.intro: Pumped hydro storage (PHS) is the world’s largest battery, using gravity to store excess renewable electricity as water potential energy. It provides fast, large‑scale balancing, making solar and wind power more reliable for grids worldwide.
learn.c1_text: When electricity is abundant, pumps move water from a lower reservoir to a higher one, storing energy as elevation. When demand rises, water flows back downhill through turbines, generating power—much like a giant rechargeable water battery.
learn.c2_text: Typical PHS plants have 1–8 GW capacity and 4–30 h of storage, with round‑trip efficiencies of 70‑85 %. They can start generating within seconds, perfect for frequency regulation and peak shaving.
learn.c3_text: India’s Ministry estimates 150 GW of viable pumped hydro sites, especially in the Himalayan foothills. The 1.5 GW Sardar Sarovar project, commissioned in 2022, supplies over 7 TWh annually to the Western grid.
learn.stat2: PHS provides 95 % of world’s bulk energy storage
learn.stat4: India’s identified PHS sites: 150 GW potential

learn.title: Green Ammonia for Shipping & Fertiliser
learn.intro: Green ammonia, made from renewable hydrogen and nitrogen, offers a carbon‑free fuel for ships and a sustainable source for fertilizers. Its high energy density and existing global trade infrastructure make it a promising bridge to a zero‑emission future.
learn.c1_text: Green ammonia is synthesized via the Haber‑Bosch process, but using green hydrogen from electrolysis instead of fossil‑derived H₂. For every tonne of ammonia, about 1.8 tonnes of CO₂ are avoided compared with grey ammonia.
learn.c2_text: Ammonia can power marine engines with similar energy content to heavy fuel oil (18 MJ/kg). It burns cleanly, emitting only nitrogen and water, and can be stored as a liquid at modest pressure, simplifying bunkering on existing vessels.
learn.c3_text: The Indian Ministry of Petroleum & Natural Gas backs a 1 GW green ammonia hub in Gujarat, targeting 0.5 Mt of production by 2030 to supply both domestic fertilizer demand (≈12 Mt/yr) and export markets.
learn.stat1: Global green ammonia capacity target: 30 Mt/yr by 2030
learn.stat3: Current worldwide ammonia production: 180 Mt/yr (mostly grey)
